lyrebird.name: Superb lyrebird
lyrebird.fact: The superb lyrebird mimics anything — other birds, camera shutters, even chainsaws.
lyrebird.author: James Ray

foxscream.name: Red fox
foxscream.fact: The red fox’s night “scream” is a contact and mating call — unsettling until you know it.
foxscream.author: Alexander Kurthy

graywolf.name: Gray wolf
graywolf.fact: A wolf pack harmonizes deliberately — each wolf shifts pitch to avoid matching another, making the pack sound larger.
graywolf.author: Data Clock

commonloon.name: Common loon
commonloon.fact: The loon’s tremolo — the “laughing” call — carries for miles across northern lakes.
commonloon.author: NPS & MSU Acoustic Atlas/Jennifer Jerrett

barnowl.name: Barn owl
barnowl.fact: The barn owl doesn’t hoot — it screeches, a raspy shriek that earned it the name “demon owl”.
barnowl.author: Luis Alvarez Menendez

cuckoo.name: Common cuckoo
cuckoo.fact: That two-note “cuck-oo” is the male; it’s also a brood parasite that never raises its own young.
cuckoo.author: Benoît Van Hecke

whippoorwill.name: Eastern whip-poor-will
whippoorwill.fact: It repeats its name into the night — sometimes hundreds of times without a pause.
whippoorwill.author: Jonathon Jongsma

barredowl.name: Barred owl
barredowl.fact: Often written “who cooks for you, who cooks for you-all?”
barredowl.author: Rob Porter

tawnyowl.name: Tawny owl
tawnyowl.fact: The classic 'twit-twoo' is a duet — the female calls 'ke-wick', the male answers 'hoo'. Two birds, one famous phrase.
tawnyowl.author: Benoît Van Hecke

greathornedowl.name: Great horned owl
greathornedowl.author: Jonathon Jongsma
greathornedowl.fact: The great horned owl's deep double-hoot — hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo — is the archetypal North American night sound, often mistaken for other owls.

nighthawk.name: Common nighthawk
nighthawk.fact: Beyond its nasal peent, the nighthawk dives and makes a boom with cupped wings — sound produced by air, not a voice.
nighthawk.author: Oliver Komar

nightingale.name: Common nightingale
nightingale.fact: The nightingale sings at night and by day — a benchmark of birdsong for centuries.
nightingale.author: GESHORS Yann

blackbird.name: Common blackbird
blackbird.fact: The common blackbird's song is always improvised — rich, flute-like, and no two males sing the same phrase twice.
blackbird.author: Benoît Van Hecke

songthrush.name: Song thrush
songthrush.fact: The song thrush repeats each phrase 2–4 times before moving on — the only British bird that composes in stanzas.
songthrush.author: Jochem verweij

europeanrobin.name: European robin
europeanrobin.fact: Robins hold territory year-round and sing through winter when almost every other bird goes silent.
europeanrobin.author: Jan Cibulka

wren.name: Eurasian wren
wren.fact: A bird the size of a ping-pong ball delivers 100+ notes in seconds. Hark’s mentor takes her name from it.
wren.author: GESHORS Yann

greattit.name: Great tit
greattit.fact: The great tit has over 40 distinct call types and creates novel variations — ornithologists call it the most vocally inventive bird in Europe.
greattit.author: Benoît Van Hecke

bluetit.name: Eurasian blue tit
bluetit.fact: The blue tit's alarm call is pitched below cat hearing but within human range — an auditory trick that misdirects predators without tipping them off.
bluetit.author: Benoît Van Hecke

chaffinch.name: Common chaffinch
chaffinch.fact: Chaffinches in different countries sing different dialects — learned from local tutors and distinct enough to identify regional origin.
chaffinch.author: Benoît Van Hecke

chiffchaff.name: Common chiffchaff
chiffchaff.fact: It names itself: chiff-chaff, chiff-chaff. One of the simplest songs in Europe, and one of the earliest harbingers of spring.
chiffchaff.author: Diana Doyle

willowwarbler.name: Willow warbler
willowwarbler.fact: A descending cascade of notes that crosses thousands of miles from sub-Saharan Africa to European springs — then returns, then repeats, for life.
willowwarbler.author: Anthony Wetherhill

skylark.name: Eurasian skylark
skylark.fact: Rises 300 metres above the ground and sings continuously — the entire ascent, the hover, the descent — a performance you can hear but can't fully follow.
skylark.author: Jens Loose

blackcap.name: Eurasian blackcap
blackcap.fact: Called the 'northern nightingale' — rich, improvised warbling that has confused birders for centuries into thinking they were hearing something rarer.
blackcap.author: Benoît Van Hecke

goldfinch.name: European goldfinch
goldfinch.fact: The European goldfinch was caged for centuries purely for its song. A foraging flock makes the liquid twittering sound known as a 'charm'.
goldfinch.author: Sander Pieterse

greenfinch.name: European greenfinch
greenfinch.fact: Its nasal wheeze sounds mechanical enough that beginners consistently mistake it for something electronic. One of the great misidentified sounds.
greenfinch.author: NobbiP

starling.name: Common starling
starling.fact: Starling flocks produce a collective sound — a rushing, crackling roar — from thousands of synchronized wingbeats. No individual is making that sound. The flock is.
starling.author: Jochem verweij

housesparrow.name: House sparrow
housesparrow.fact: Once the most common bird in every city on Earth, now in serious decline. The silence spreading into cities used to be constant sparrow noise.
housesparrow.author: Jonathon Jongsma

hoopoe.name: Eurasian hoopoe
hoopoe.fact: Its low 'hoo-poo-poo' carries far enough to be mistaken for owls. A returning migrant from Africa, and one of the most recognized calls in European folklore.
hoopoe.author: paulo kivuyo

kingfisher.name: Common kingfisher
kingfisher.fact: Its sharp whistle is heard more often than the bird is seen — a streak of iridescent blue moving at 40 mph below the waterline.
kingfisher.author: Sharadapte

curlew.name: Eurasian curlew
curlew.fact: The curlew's bubbling, rising call is among the most haunting sounds on Earth. Its population has declined 50% since the 1970s.
curlew.author: Pascal Christe

commoncrane.name: Common crane
commoncrane.fact: Cranes produce their resonating, bugling call through a trachea coiled inside the sternum — a biological horn nearly five feet long.
commoncrane.author: Pálos Lőrinc

americanrobin.name: American robin
americanrobin.fact: The American robin's 'cheerily, cheer-up, cheerio' is called the first sign of spring — though it overwinters nearby all along.
americanrobin.author: Jonathon Jongsma

cardinal.name: Northern cardinal
cardinal.fact: Only the female northern cardinal sings a complex territorial song. Males are flashier; females are the better vocalists.
cardinal.author: Jonathon Jongsma

chickadee.name: Black-capped chickadee
chickadee.fact: The black-capped chickadee encodes threat level in its alarm — more dee's means bigger predator. 'Chickadee-dee-dee-DEE' is a hawk warning.
chickadee.author: Jonathon Jongsma

bluejay.name: Blue jay
bluejay.fact: Blue jays mimic red-tailed hawk calls to clear feeders of competitors. They also scout danger and relay alarm calls more accurately than most species around them.
bluejay.author: Jonathon Jongsma

americancrow.name: American crow
americancrow.fact: American crows have regional dialects — a crow from New York sounds different from one in California, and they teach those dialects to their young.
americancrow.author: Jonathon Jongsma

raven.name: Common raven
raven.fact: Ravens use something approaching grammar — distinct 'words' for aerial versus ground predators, combined in ways that change meaning.
raven.author: NPS/David Restivo

redwingedblackbird.name: Red-winged blackbird
redwingedblackbird.fact: Male red-winged blackbirds average 600 songs per hour during breeding season — one of the most heard sounds in North American wetlands.
redwingedblackbird.author: Jonathon Jongsma

woodthrush.name: Wood thrush
woodthrush.fact: The wood thrush sings two notes simultaneously using two separate voice chambers — a single bird producing audible harmony.
woodthrush.author: Jonathon Jongsma

hermitthrush.name: Hermit thrush
hermitthrush.fact: Widely considered the finest singer in North America. Its phrases sit a perfect fourth apart — naturally harmonic, as if the bird knows music theory.
hermitthrush.author: Leonardo Guzman Hernandez

carolinawren.name: Carolina wren
carolinawren.fact: One of the loudest birds on Earth relative to body weight. A bird the size of your fist that fills an entire yard.
carolinawren.author: Ryan Hodnett

housewren.name: House wren
housewren.fact: House wrens build 'dummy nests' — filling every available cavity in their territory to prevent competitors from moving in. Song is the advertisement.
housewren.author: Dario Taraborelli

americangoldfinch.name: American goldfinch
americangoldfinch.fact: One of the only birds that waits until late summer to nest — timed precisely to the ripening of thistle seeds, fed exclusively to young.
americangoldfinch.author: Kevin Hood

indigobunting.name: Indigo bunting
indigobunting.fact: Young male indigo buntings have no innate song — they learn it entirely from a nearby 'tutor' male. Populations have distinct local songs passed across generations.
indigobunting.author: Jonathon Jongsma

ovenbird.name: Ovenbird
ovenbird.fact: 'Tea-CHER tea-CHER tea-CHER' — climbs in both volume and pitch to carry through dense forest canopy without loss.
ovenbird.author: Zachary Millen

commonyellowthroat.name: Common yellowthroat
commonyellowthroat.fact: The male's 'witchety-witchety-witchety' is one of North America's most distinctive warblers. Females select mates in part by vocal complexity.
commonyellowthroat.author: Jonathon Jongsma

yellowwarbler.name: Yellow warbler
yellowwarbler.fact: One of the most studied neotropical migrants. Its song is the textbook example of warbler song structure taught to every birder.
yellowwarbler.author: Niels Krabbe

baltimoreoriole.name: Baltimore oriole
baltimoreoriole.author: Jonathon Jongsma
baltimoreoriole.fact: The Baltimore oriole constructs hanging, woven nests up to a foot deep — and its song is the announcement that construction season has begun.

easternmeadowlark.name: Eastern meadowlark
easternmeadowlark.fact: Its clear, whistled song carries half a mile across open grassland. Meadowlark populations have declined 70% as the grasslands they need disappear.
easternmeadowlark.author: Jonathon Jongsma

easternphoebe.name: Eastern phoebe
easternphoebe.fact: The first bird species banded in North America — Audubon tied thread to its leg in 1804 and confirmed it returned the following year.
easternphoebe.author: Jonathon Jongsma

graycatbird.name: Gray catbird
graycatbird.fact: Named for its cat-like mew when disturbed, but it sings an endless, improvised medley that never repeats itself within a session.
graycatbird.author: Jonathon Jongsma

brownthrasher.name: Brown thrasher
brownthrasher.fact: The brown thrasher has the largest known song repertoire of any North American bird — over 1,100 distinct phrases, each sung twice before moving on.
brownthrasher.author: Jonathon Jongsma

cedarwaxwing.name: Cedar waxwing
cedarwaxwing.fact: Cedar waxwings pass berries beak-to-beak down a line of birds — one of the few species documented practicing food sharing as social behaviour.
cedarwaxwing.author: Jonathon Jongsma

whitethroatedsparrow.name: White-throated sparrow
whitethroatedsparrow.fact: 'Old Sam Peabody, Peabody, Peabody' — the mnemonic birders use for its call has been passed between teachers for over a century.
whitethroatedsparrow.author: Vidian

songsparrow.name: Song sparrow
songsparrow.fact: Song sparrows vary their tempo and phrase order to avoid listeners habituating — they notice when you stop responding and switch songs.
songsparrow.author: Jonathon Jongsma

junco.name: Dark-eyed junco
junco.fact: The dark-eyed junco is one of the most studied wild birds on Earth — 70 million individuals used as a living environmental sensor by ecologists.
junco.author: Jonathon Jongsma

commongrackle.name: Common grackle
commongrackle.fact: Grackles have a hard ridge inside the bill that functions as a grain cracker. Their iridescent flocks and grating calls are the soundtrack of North American springs.
commongrackle.author: Leonardo Guzman Hernandez

sandhillcrane.name: Sandhill crane
sandhillcrane.fact: Their bugling call is produced by a trachea coiled inside the chest — six feet long in adults. One of the oldest bird sounds on Earth: cranes have been calling like this for ten million years.
sandhillcrane.author: NPS & MSU Acoustic Atlas / Jennifer Jerrett

killdeer.name: Killdeer
killdeer.fact: Named for its alarm — 'kill-deer, kill-deer'. If a predator approaches its nest, the killdeer performs an elaborate broken-wing display to lead it away.
killdeer.author: NPS/Peter Comley

pileatedwoodpecker.name: Pileated woodpecker
pileatedwoodpecker.fact: The largest woodpecker in North America — excavates rectangular holes up to 18 inches. Its laughing call directly inspired the Woody Woodpecker character.
pileatedwoodpecker.author: Jonathon Jongsma

northernflicker.name: Northern flicker
northernflicker.fact: Unlike most woodpeckers, flickers drum on resonant metal surfaces — gutters, street signs, satellite dishes — for maximum carry.
northernflicker.author: *ngoomie

australianmagpie.name: Australian magpie
australianmagpie.fact: One of the most complex bird songs on Earth — caroling, improvised, and often performed in overlapping groups. Australians grow up hearing it and don't realize it's extraordinary until they leave.
australianmagpie.author: James Ray

reddeer.name: Red deer
reddeer.fact: A red deer stag can bellow over a thousand times in a single day during the rut — using a descended larynx it grew specifically for this purpose.
reddeer.author: 32-Fuß-Freak

treefrog.name: Pacific tree frog
treefrog.fact: The classic “movie frog” — Hollywood used its two-part call everywhere.
treefrog.author: James Ray

springpeeper.name: Spring peeper
springpeeper.fact: A chorus of spring peepers is one of the loudest signs of spring — each tiny frog louder, pound for pound, than a power tool.
springpeeper.author: Ryan Hodnett

greenfrog.name: Green frog
greenfrog.fact: The male green frog's call sounds like a plucked banjo string. Found at the edges of ponds and slow streams — rarely seen, reliably heard.
greenfrog.author: Jonathon Jongsma

woodfrog.name: Wood frog
woodfrog.fact: The wood frog is the only amphibian found north of the Arctic Circle. Its spring eruption of calling — from frozen vernal pools — is one of nature's most sudden mass events.
woodfrog.author: Ryan Hodnett

graytreefrog.name: Gray treefrog
graytreefrog.fact: The gray treefrog has antifreeze compounds in its blood. It can survive being frozen solid and thaw back to life without harm.
graytreefrog.author: Alysonann

americantoad.name: American toad
americantoad.fact: The American toad sustains its trill for up to 30 seconds without pause. Males in a chorus choose pitches that don't overlap with neighbors — acoustic zoning.
americantoad.author: SwampVids

commonfrog.name: Common frog
commonfrog.fact: Europe's most familiar amphibian produces a quiet, purring croak — often the first frog sound people learn. Its populations have been quietly declining for decades.
commonfrog.author: Baudewijn Odé

natterjack.name: Natterjack toad
natterjack.fact: The natterjack toad runs rather than hops, and its mating call — the loudest of any British amphibian — carries for nearly two miles.
natterjack.author: Christian Fischer

canetoad.name: Cane toad
canetoad.fact: Introduced worldwide as a pest control solution, the cane toad became one of the most ecologically destructive invasives on Earth. It calls through the night, indifferent.
canetoad.author: forta cienpo

cicada.name: Cicada
cicada.fact: A cicada drums with tymbals — ribbed membranes it buckles hundreds of times a second.
cicada.author: Subhashish Panigrahi

fieldcricket.name: Field cricket
fieldcricket.fact: The cricket chirp is produced by drawing the edge of one wing across a comb of microscopic teeth on the other — an instrument called a stridulatory file.

treecricket.name: Snowy tree cricket
treecricket.fact: The snowy tree cricket chirps at a rate that tracks air temperature — count its chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 to get the Fahrenheit reading.
treecricket.author: Olivier Grimm

katydid.name: Katydid
katydid.fact: Katydids have ears on their forelegs — tiny slits that detect ultrasound. Their own calls have frequency content well above human hearing.
katydid.author: Gunther Tschuch

molecricket.name: Mole cricket
molecricket.fact: The mole cricket excavates a double-mouthed burrow shaped specifically to amplify and project its call — a biological acoustic horn of its own construction.
molecricket.author: Olga Korsunovskaya

honeybee.name: Honey bee
honeybee.fact: A foraging honeybee's 300 Hz wing vibration carries information to hivemates on dance floors — the waggle dance is communication through sound and movement.
honeybee.author: YleArkisto

bushcricket.name: Bush cricket
bushcricket.fact: Most bush cricket calls are above human hearing — we hear only the lowest harmonics of a richer signal we can't access without instruments.

thunder.name: Thunder
thunder.fact: Thunder is the sound of air exploding outward from a lightning channel — superheated to 30,000°C in microseconds, then collapsing back to ambient pressure.
thunder.author: Richard Humphries

oceanwaves.name: Ocean waves
oceanwaves.fact: The low-frequency rumble of breaking waves — called microseismic noise — is the only sound that the entire Earth produces continuously, everywhere, at all times.
oceanwaves.author: U.S. Merchant Marine Academy Band

stream.name: Forest stream
stream.fact: Moving water carries all frequencies simultaneously. Field recordists use streams as acoustic baselines — anything living cuts through that background.
stream.author: Jarrod stanley

wind.name: Wind
wind.fact: Wind is geophony — sound with no mouth. Learning to hear past it, and through it, is the first skill every field recordist builds.
wind.author: Robert EA Harvey

kookaburra.name: Laughing kookaburra
kookaburra.fact: The kookaburra's laugh marks territory, not amusement — but knowing that makes it no less infectious when it erupts at dawn.
kookaburra.author: James Ray

mockingbird.name: Northern mockingbird
mockingbird.fact: The northern mockingbird memorizes every sound it hears across a lifetime and plays them back in medleys. Its archive grows until the day it dies.
mockingbird.author: Sandtouch Limited Company

tuftedtitmouse.name: Tufted titmouse
tuftedtitmouse.fact: The tufted titmouse has a vocabulary complex enough that researchers can count individuals in a forest by call alone — without ever seeing the birds.
tuftedtitmouse.author: steve

morepork.name: Morepork
morepork.fact: Named for its call: 'more-pork', clearly and repeatedly. The only owl native to New Zealand, known in Māori as ruru.
morepork.author: craig wilson

redsquirrel.name: American red squirrel
redsquirrel.fact: The American red squirrel's scolding alarm call distinguishes aerial from ground predators — two distinct calls that trigger two different escape responses from every species within earshot.
redsquirrel.author: Doug Hynes

coyote.name: Coyote
coyote.fact: A single coyote can sound like three or four animals using rapid pitch changes within one howl. The yip-howl chorus is coordinated — a broadcast of pack size and location.
coyote.author: Doug Hynes

tui.name: Tūī
tui.fact: The tui clicks, coughs, wheezes, and cascades — an improvised performance that incorporates frequencies above and below human hearing, sometimes at the same time.
tui.author: craig wilson

mourningdove.name: Mourning dove
mourningdove.fact: The mourning dove's low, mournful coo is made on the inhale as well as the exhale — giving it a continuous, uninterrupted quality unlike most bird calls.
mourningdove.author: Jonathon Jongsma

engine.name: Distant engine
engine.fact: Engine drone is anthrophony — human noise. Telling it from biophony is a core field skill.
engine.author: Wojciech Walkusz (WWS)

rain.name: Summer rain
rain.fact: Rain is geophony — the percussion of weather. A trained ear can estimate rainfall intensity just from the acoustic texture.
rain.author: Robert EA Harvey
